records.ety: [OE. stiren, steren, sturen, AS. styrian; probably akin to D. storen to disturb, G. stören, OHG. stōren to scatter, destroy. √166.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Stirred (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Stirring.]

defs: To change the place of in any manner; to move.
quotes: My foot I had never yet in five days been able to stir.

defs: To disturb the relative position of the particles of, as of a liquid, by passing something through it; to agitate; as, to stir a pudding with a spoon.
quotes: My mind is troubled, like a fountain stirred.

defs: To bring into debate; to agitate; to moot.
To incite to action; to arouse; to instigate; to prompt; to excite.
quotes: An Ate, stirring him to blood and strife.
And for her sake some mutiny will stir.
notes: ☞ In all senses except the first, stir is often followed by up with an intensive effect; as, to stir up fire; to stir up sedition.

defs: To move; to change one's position.
quotes: I had not power to stir or strive, But felt that I was still alive.

defs: To be in motion; to be active or bustling; to exert or busy one's self.
quotes: All are not fit with them to stir and toil.
The friends of the unfortunate exile, far from resenting his unjust suspicions, were stirring anxiously in his behalf.

defs: To become the object of notice; to be on foot.
quotes: They fancy they have a right to talk freely upon everything that stirs or appears.

defs: To rise, or be up, in the morning.
The act or result of stirring; agitation; tumult; bustle; noise or various movements.
quotes: Why all these words, this clamor, and this stir?
Consider, after so much stir about genus and species, how few words we have yet settled definitions of.

defs: Public disturbance or commotion; tumultuous disorder; seditious uproar.
quotes: Being advertised of some stirs raised by his unnatural sons in England.
